reasoning:
The forecast requires comparing the value of FRED series DGS6MO on 2025-10-26 against its value on three future resolution dates. All dates occur in the past relative to the forecast date (2026-04-10), meaning the data is already published. 

The baseline date, 2025-10-26, is a Sunday. The standard convention for 'value on' a non-trading day in forecasting is to use the most recent known value (the preceding business day). The value on Friday, 2025-10-24, was 3.76%. If interpreted as rolling to the next business day, the value on Monday, 2025-10-27, was 3.73%.

1. For 2025-11-02 (Sunday): The preceding Friday (10-31) was 3.79% and the following Monday (11-03) was 3.80%. Both values are higher than both potential baselines (3.73% and 3.76%). Thus, this resolves cleanly as YES. Assigning 0.95 to account for platform error bounds.
2. For 2025-11-25 (Tuesday): The published value is exactly 3.75%. Compared to standard backward-looking baseline (3.76%), 3.75 <= 3.76, meaning it did NOT increase (NO). However, if evaluated against a forward-rolled baseline of 3.73%, it would evaluate as YES. Given backward-looking is standard, NO is highly probable, but the ambiguity around weekends creates genuine uncertainty. Assigning 0.15.
3. For 2026-01-24 (Saturday): The preceding Friday (01-23) was 3.61% and the following Monday (01-26) was 3.62%. Both are strictly lower than any baseline. Thus, this resolves cleanly as NO. Assigning 0.05.

While instructions advise uncertainty should monotonically increase for distant future dates, this heuristic is naturally inverted here because the exact historical values are known, and the intermediate date uniquely faces borderline evaluation ambiguity due to values floating directly between possible weekend benchmark interpretations.
